questions.text: subTags: Subject-Verb Agreement
questions.subject: English

options.text: Della knew that her hair were more beautiful than any queen's jewels and gifts.
Della knew that her hairs were more beautiful than any queen's jewels and gifts.
Della knew that her hair was more beautiful than any queen's jewels and gifts.
options.explanation: Subject-Verb Agreement: "Hair" is a singular noun, so the verb "was" is the correct singular form.Possessive Form: The possessive form "queen's" is correctly used to indicate possession.Clear and Correct Comparison: The sentence effectively compares Della's hair to any queen's jewels and gifts in a clear and grammatically correct manner.Therefore, option C is the grammatically accurate choice.

options.text: Della knew that her hair was more beautiful than any queens jewels and gifts.

questions.explanation: Subject-Verb Agreement: "Hair" is a singular noun, so the verb "was" is the correct singular form.Possessive Form: The possessive form "queen's" is correctly used to indicate possession.Clear and Correct Comparison: The sentence effectively compares Della's hair to any queen's jewels and gifts in a clear and grammatically correct manner.Therefore, option C is the grammatically accurate choice.

questions.text: Subtags: Basic Rules and Concepts, Subject-Verb Agreement with Quantifiers and Phrases

options.text: Every pupil, together with staff and old friends of school, is taking his turn at ringing the bells.
options.explanation: Subject-Verb Agreement: The subject is "Every pupil, together with staff and old friends of school," which is a singular compound subject. The verb "is" correctly agrees with the singular compound subject.Pronoun Agreement: The pronoun "his" agrees with the singular subject "Every pupil."

The verb "is" correctly agrees with the singular compound subject.Pronoun Agreement: The pronoun "his" agrees with the singular subject "Every pupil."

options.text: Neither Bill's parents nor his wife has mentioned anything about moving house.
options.explanation: Subject-Verb Agreement: The compound subject "Neither Bill's parents nor his wife" is singular, and the correct verb is "has" to agree with the singular subject.Clear and Correct Comparison: The sentence effectively uses "neither...nor" to convey a negative conjunction, and the verb "has" agrees with the singular subject.

questions.text: 26 Subtags: Basic Rules and Concepts, Exception and Special Cases
questions.subject: English

options.text: Mrs. Jones stopped, jerked him around in front of her put a half nelson about his neck, and continued to drag it up the street.
Mrs. Jones stopped, jerked him around in front of her put a half nelson about his neck, and continued to drag up the street.
Mrs. Jones stopped, jerked him around in front of her put a half nelson about his neck, and continued to drag him up the street.
options.explanation: Parallel Structure: The verbs in the series of actions are parallel, maintaining consistency in the structure.Pronoun Clarity: The use of "him" as the object of the action is clear and appropriate.Comma Usage: Commas are appropriately used to separate items in the series of actions.Therefore, Option C is the correct and grammatically sound choice.

options.text: Mrs. Jones stopped, jerked him around in front of her put a half nelson about his neck, and continued to drag himself up the street.

questions.explanation: Parallel Structure: The verbs in the series of actions are parallel, maintaining consistency in the structure.Pronoun Clarity: The use of "him" as the object of the action is clear and appropriate.Comma Usage: Commas are appropriately used to separate items in the series of actions.Therefore, Option C is the correct and grammatically sound choice.

questions.text: Subtags: Basic Rules and Concepts, Exception and Special Cases
questions.subject: English

options.text: Not only have our ways of living changed, but people themselves have also been changed.
options.explanation: Subject-Verb Agreement: The subject "our ways of living" is plural, so the verb "have" is appropriately plural to maintain agreement.Correlative Conjunction: "Not only" is a correlative conjunction, and when used at the beginning of a sentence, it requires an inversion in the word order. The correct form is "Not only have...but also."Clear Thought: The sentence effectively conveys a clear and complete thought, expressing a parallel relationship between the changes in ways of living and the changes in people themselves.

options.text: Not only has our ways of living changed, but people themselves have also been changed.
Not only our ways of living have changed, but people themselves have also been changed.
Not only have our way of living changed, but people themselves have also been changed.

questions.explanation: Subject-Verb Agreement: The subject "our ways of living" is plural, so the verb "have" is appropriately plural to maintain agreement.Correlative Conjunction: "Not only" is a correlative conjunction, and when used at the beginning of a sentence, it requires an inversion in the word order. The correct form is "Not only have...but also."Clear Thought: The sentence effectively conveys a clear and complete thought, expressing a parallel relationship between the changes in ways of living and the changes in people themselves.

options.explanation: While colloquially used, "It is him" is considered less formal and may not be suitable for more formal contexts.

In formal English, especially in a predicate nominative (the subject complement), the pronoun "he" is preferred over "him." Therefore, the correct phrasing is "It is he." This choice maintains grammatical correctness.
"It is his" is grammatically incorrect in this context. The correct pronoun to use after the linking verb "is" is the subject form "he."
The reflexive pronoun "himself" is not appropriate in this context. The correct form after the linking verb "is" is the subject form "he."
